original_abstract: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) remain two of the most significant retroviral threats to feline health globally, often leading to severe immunosuppression, secondary infections, and reduced life expectancy. This study provides the first comprehensive assessment of FIV and FeLV prevalence specifically among shelter cats in Portugal, a population at high risk due to unknown medical histories and communal living conditions. Blood samples from 326 stray cats were collected during routine intake examinations at an official shelter in northeast Portugal between 2017 and 2023. Using point-of-care immunochromatographic testing, we identified an overall seroprevalence of 15.3% (50/326). When analyzed by specific pathogen, 9.8% of cats were positive for FIV alone, 2.1% for FeLV alone, and 3.4% were co-infected with both viruses. We found that adult cats were significantly more likely to test positive for FIV and co-infections than kittens, though other demographic factors—such as sex, breed, hair length, and municipality—showed no statistical association with infection rates. These results indicate that while FeLV remains present at a stable low level, FIV prevalence in Portuguese shelters is notably elevated compared to other European regions. Our findings underscore a critical need for universal retroviral screening upon shelter admission, more robust public vaccination efforts, and the implementation of vigilant management strategies to mitigate viral transmission among Portugal’s stray and domestic cat populations.
humanized_abstract: Feline immunodeficiency virus (FIV) and feline leukaemia virus (FeLV) are two of the major retroviral diseases affecting felines worldwide and can lead to immunosuppression, disease secondary to opportunistic infections, and a reduced lifespan. There has been no study done to date regarding the prevalence of FIV/FeLV in shelter cats in Portugal. Cats in shelters often have unknown medical history and can lead precarious lifestyles, thus representing a high-risk population for the exposure and transmission of FIV/FeLV infections. The aim of the present study was to assess retrospectively the prevalence of FIV/FeLV among a population of 326 stray cats admitted in a official shelter in the north-eastern Portugal over a period of six years (2017–2023). Blood samples were obtained during the intake examinations, and retroviral infections were detected by point-of-care immunochromatographic test (antibody detection). Overall, the FIV/FeLV seroprevalence in this specific shelter cat population was 15.3% (50/326). Out of these, 9.8% of the cats were FIV positive while 2.1% were FeLV positive and 3.4% were co-infected by both viruses. FIV infections were recorded exclusively in adult cats and thus a higher prevalence was detected among this age group compared with kittens, whereas gender, breed, coat length and municipality of origin were not associated with FIV or co-infection infection in the shelter population. These results show a relatively low FeLV prevalence and stable rates of the past two decades, contrasting with an elevated FIV prevalence recorded among shelter cats in Portugal compared with that described in other European countries. These findings suggest the urgent need for the implementation of a universal retroviral testing policy at shelter entrances, improvement of community vaccination initiatives, and enhanced viral surveillance measures in both feral and domestic cat populations in Portugal.
